subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
Exact component counts from the rulebook:
- 2 Screens
- 48 Code Cards total (1 black deck of 24 cards, 1 white deck of 24 cards)
- 110 Keyword Cards (440 words total)
- 4 Interception Tokens (white)
- 4 Miscommunication Tokens (black)
- 1 Sand Timer (30 seconds)
- 50 Note Sheets
- 1 Rulebook

Keyword card handling note from rulebook: do not pre-shuffle or pre-read keyword cards before play.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
1) Split players into two teams as evenly as possible (normally 2-4 players per team; 3-player variant exists in the rulebook).
2) Teams sit opposite each other, with teammates together.
3) Each team takes 1 Screen.
4) Each team draws 4 Keyword Cards and places them into the 4 numbered slots of their Screen so only that team can see them.
5) Each team takes the Code deck matching their screen color (Black team gets black deck, White team gets white deck).
6) Each team takes 1 Note Sheet and writes team name.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
Place the Sand Timer, Interception tokens, and Miscommunication tokens in the center of the table.

Choose an initial Encryptor for each team (this role rotates each round).

Round flow always starts with the White Team’s code sequence, then the Black Team’s.

Important round-1 condition: during the first round, neither team attempts interception of the opposing code. They still decode their own Encryptor’s clues as normal.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
The game is played over rounds (usually 4-6). Each round follows this strict order:
1. Each team’s Encryptor secretly draws 1 Code Card and reads it privately.
2. Each Encryptor writes 3 clues (top line for first digit, middle for second, bottom for third) on their Note Sheet.
3. White Encryptor reads White clues aloud; Black team records them on white side of their note sheet.
4. Both teams discuss and write their attempted White code in the first response column (Encryptor is silent and gives no reactions).
5. Black team states interception attempt for White code (except round 1, when interception is skipped).
6. White team states its own decode attempt.
7. White Encryptor reveals actual code card; assign tokens if interception and/or miscommunication occurred.
8. Repeat steps 3-7 for Black team (Black clues spoken, both teams guess, White may intercept except round 1, Black attempts own decode, reveal).
9. End of round: check win/loss. If game continues, return used code cards to respective decks, shuffle, and rotate Encryptor on each team.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Encryptor clue rules (must follow all):
- You may give clues as a word, phrase, sentence, sound, hum, dance, or mime, as long as the 3 clues are clearly separated in order.
- Clues must refer to keyword meaning only.
- Forbidden clue methods: spelling hints, letter counts, position hints (e.g., referencing keyword slot number), pronunciation tricks.
- Clues must rely only on publicly available information; no private shared memories or private references.
- If asked, you must spell clues aloud.
- Any information given to your team must equally be available to opponents (public communication only).
- Once spoken, a clue cannot be modified or improved.
- You may never repeat the same clue in the same game.
- You may never read code digits aloud, and may not use the keyword itself (or direct translation) as a clue, even for another keyword.

Team decoding/interception actions:
- Teams discuss clues and map each clue to a keyword number (1-4).
- Teams write a 3-digit attempt in order.
- Interception succeeds only with exact digits in exact order.
- Own-team decode fails if any digit/order is wrong, causing a Miscommunication token.
- It is possible in the same reveal for one team to gain an Interception token while the clue-giving team gains a Miscommunication token.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Persistent information leakage:
- Keyword assignments stay constant for the entire game.
- Because clues are spoken aloud and logged, both teams build a growing clue history for each keyword number. This is the main strategic engine of the game.

Note Sheet system:
- First response column records attempted codes.
- Second response column records correct code after reveal.
- Bottom grouping boxes collect clue history by keyword number, helping long-term deduction.

Optional time pressure:
- To speed play, once either Encryptor finishes writing clues, flip sand timer. The other Encryptor must finish before sand runs out, or they may end up with fewer than 3 complete clues.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
A team can win/lose during end-of-round checks:
- Win condition: your team has 2 Interception tokens.
- Loss condition: your team has 2 Miscommunication tokens.

If at least one such condition is met at end of the round, game ends and evaluate tiebreaker situations as needed.

Tiebreaker is required if any of these occur:
1) A team has both 2 Interception and 2 Miscommunication tokens at end of round (simultaneous win/loss).
2) Both teams gain their second Interception token in the same round.
3) Both teams gain their second Miscommunication token in the same round.
4) Neither team has won/lost by end of round 8.

Tiebreak procedure:
- Score tokens: each Interception token = +1 point; each Miscommunication token = -1 point.
- Team with higher total wins.
- If tied, each team guesses the opposing team’s 4 keywords; team with more correct guesses wins.
- If still tied, both teams share victory.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Establish stable clue lanes in rounds 1-2.
Use clues that are understandable to your teammates but broad enough that opponents cannot instantly map exact keywords.

2) Record everything cleanly.
The Note Sheet is your advantage engine. Write opponent clues accurately and group them by likely keyword number early.

3) Prioritize teammate reliability over cleverness.
A safe clue your teammate gets is better than a brilliant clue they miss (which gives you a Miscommunication token).

4) Start interception modeling immediately after round 1.
Even though no round-1 interception occurs, those first clues become your baseline map for future steals.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Giving overly specific clues too early.
This makes your keyword mapping obvious and fuels enemy interceptions by round 2-3.

2) Being too vague with your own team.
If your teammate cannot consistently connect clue to the right number, you hand out Miscommunication tokens.

3) Reusing clue words.
Rule violation risk aside, repeated terms make your pattern transparent and predictable.

4) Encryptor reacting during team discussion.
Even subtle facial reactions break the information rules and compromise fairness.

5) Tracking opponent clues poorly.
Teams lose many games by forgetting clue history and then missing obvious interceptions.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Decision 1: Clarity vs secrecy
- More clarity helps your team decode accurately now.
- More secrecy slows opponent interception later.
Balancing these is the core skill of Decrypto.

Decision 2: Conservative decode vs risky interception
- Conservative calls avoid self-inflicted errors.
- Aggressive interception attempts can win quickly if your clue model is strong.

Decision 3: Short-term round result vs long-term information control
- A clue that wins this round may reveal too much for future rounds.
- Sometimes a slightly less direct clue protects your keyword map for the rest of the game.

metadata.notes: Written directly from provided Decrypto rulebook excerpt, including token scoring and round-order details.
